title: Chain-of-Agents: End-to-End Agent Foundation Models via Multi-Agent Distillation and Agentic RL
summary: This paper introduces Chain-of-Agents (CoA), a novel paradigm enabling large language models to solve complex problems end-to-end by simulating multi-agent collaboration within a single model. It trains these Agent Foundation Models (AFMs) through a two-step process: distilling knowledge from existing multi-agent systems and applying agentic reinforcement learning on verifiable tasks. AFMs achieve state-of-the-art performance in web and code agent benchmarks, with all research components made open-source.
key_findings: Introduction of Chain-of-Agents (CoA), a paradigm for end-to-end multi-agent problem-solving within a single LLM by dynamically activating tool and role-playing agents.
Development of Agent Foundation Models (AFMs) that overcome the inefficiencies and limitations of manual prompt/workflow engineering in existing multi-agent systems.
A novel two-stage training framework combining multi-agent distillation for supervised fine-tuning and agentic reinforcement learning on verifiable tasks.
AFMs establish new state-of-the-art performance across diverse web agent and code agent benchmarks.
The entire research, including model weights, code, and training data, is fully open-sourced, providing a foundation for future agent model research.
methodology: The paper proposes training Agent Foundation Models (AFMs) using a multi-agent distillation framework to generate chain-of-agents trajectories for supervised fine-tuning, followed by agentic reinforcement learning on verifiable tasks to further improve end-to-end problem-solving capabilities.
bias_disclaimer: AI-generated analysis may contain inaccuracies or reflect biases from the underlying model. Always perform critical evaluation.

research_gaps: The abstract does not detail the computational resources required for the multi-agent distillation and agentic reinforcement learning, which could be a practical limitation for scaling.
The abstract does not discuss the interpretability or debuggability of the dynamic agent activation within a single model compared to more explicit multi-agent frameworks.
